story_title: Peter Munk Builds Barrick — From a Failed Electronics Company to the World's Largest Gold Miner
summary: Hungarian-born entrepreneur Peter Munk pivoted from a bankrupt electronics business to gold mining in 1983 by acquiring Barrick Resources Corp., an obscure Canadian junior; within 25 years he had built it into Barrick Gold, the world's largest gold mining company, through a series of audacious acquisitions including the Goldstrike mine in Nevada and the $10.4 billion takeover of Placer Dome.
recommended_arc: Hook: a disgraced entrepreneur blacklisted from Canadian business buys a nothing gold company for next to nothing. Rising: the Goldstrike find transforms Barrick into the world's lowest-cost producer. Climax: the $10.4 billion Placer Dome acquisition makes Barrick undeniably the world's largest. Payoff: Munk's net worth hits $1 billion; Barrick's Toronto headquarters bears his name.

verified_facts.claim: Peter Munk, a Hungarian-born Canadian entrepreneur who had been banned from trading on Canadian stock exchanges following the collapse of his electronics company Clairtone in the 1970s, acquired a controlling interest in Barrick Resources Corp. in 1983 with a small group of investors including Saudi arms dealer Adnan Khashoggi.
Barrick Resources was renamed Barrick Gold Corporation in 1984; at the time of the name change the company was a minor exploration-stage firm with no producing mines.

Barrick's acquisition of the Goldstrike mine on the Carlin Trend in Nevada in 1986 for approximately $62 million — considered absurdly cheap at the time — proved transformative; subsequent exploration revealed Goldstrike to contain over 30 million ounces of gold, making it one of the largest gold deposits in US history.

By the mid-1990s, Barrick's Goldstrike operations had made it one of the world's lowest-cost gold producers, with all-in production costs reportedly below $200 per ounce when the gold price was hovering around $380 per ounce.

In January 2006, Barrick Gold completed the acquisition of Placer Dome — one of the world's largest gold miners — for approximately C$10.4 billion (US$9.2 billion), the largest takeover in gold mining history at that time, catapulting Barrick to the undisputed position of world's largest gold producer.

At the time of Peter Munk's retirement as Barrick's executive chairman in 2014, Barrick Gold had a market capitalisation of approximately $15 billion, owned mines across five continents, and produced roughly 7.2 million ounces of gold per year.
